pages.text: Mechanical and dynamic properties of self-compacting crumb rubber modiﬁed concrete Khalid B. Najim ⇑, Matthew R. Hall Nottingham Centre for Geomechanics, Division of Materials, Mechanics and Structures, Faculty of Engineering, University of Nottingham, Universi ty Park, Nottingham NG7 2RD, UK article info Article history: Received 28 February 2011 Received in revised form 24 June 2011 Accepted 18 July 2011 Available online 23 August 2011 Keywords: Self-compacting concrete Alternative aggregates Crumb rubber Dynamic properties Vibration Mechanical properties abstract In this study, the mechanical and dynamic properties of Self-Compacting Rubberised Concrete (SCRC) were experimentally investigated. Crumb rubber from scrap tyres was used as a partial replacement for Fine Aggregate (FA), Coarse Aggregate (CA) and combined Fine and Coarse Aggregate (FCA) at 5, 10, and 15 wt% proportions. Incorporating rubber aggregates generally has a detrimental effect on the mechanical strength, but gives improved strain capacity resulting in signiﬁcant reductions in the ﬂexural Crack Mouth Open Displacement (CMOD), compared to the reference mix. Structural grade SCRC (f c > 17 MPa; q > 2000 kg/m3) can be obtained with up to /C24 260 kg/m3 (equivalent to 15 wt%) in all forms of crumb rubber aggregate substitution. The Dynamic Modulus and Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity decreased as the proportion of rubber substitution was increased, however SCRC has superior vibration damping behaviour in all cases with up to 230% enhancement in damping ratio and damping coefﬁcient for the CR 15 wt% mix. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Self-Compacting Concrete (SCC) is a sophisticated high perfor- mance concrete [1] described as the ‘‘ most revolutionary step ’’ in concrete technology over the last two decades due to its impact on economic and environmental sustainability in the construction industry [2]. It offers the advantages of increased productivity rates, decreased manpower, and elimination of the noise and fuel consumption associated with vibrator plant [3]. In comparison with plain concrete, it has the ability to fully self-compact under its own weight [4–9] and has high ﬂowability and ﬁlling rates, re- duced blocking in congested reinforced areas, and high segregation resistance [10], as well as high durability, low permeability and high compressive strength [11–13]. Recycling end-of-life vehicle tyres as alternative aggregates to produce a new class concrete is an innovative option with environ- mental, economic and performance beneﬁts [14]. Using shredded and/or crumb rubber particles as concrete aggregate replacement has been widely researched (summarised in a detailed review pa- per [14]), and several recent studies explore the increase in com- patibility of crumb rubber particles when used as a sand replacement in SCC mixes [14–20]. Due to the high powder content of SCC its microstructure is very compacted and dense, which re- sults in high mechanical strength and brittle failure modes. There- fore, Self-Compacting Rubberised Concrete (SCRC) composites could be used for applications requiring deformable (high ductil- ity) concrete with high ﬂowability and low/medium strength (<35 MPa), which is difﬁcult to achieve using SCC [20,21] using alternative aggregates that are environmentally friendly (i.e. green-concrete). In infrastructure applications the range of strength is usually between 28 and 35 MPa. In applications such as bridge ba

Ltd. in Manchester, UK, see Fig. 1 . Aggregate particle-size distribution was deter- mined in accordance with BS 882:1992 [24] and is presented in Fig. 2. The chemical composition of the crumb rubber is given in Table 1, and its physical properties are summarised in Table 2 . Pulverised Fuel Ash (PFA) was sourced from Ratcliffe-on- Soar power station and was used a pozzolanic cement additive. The chemical com- position of the PFA is given in Table 3 , and this conforms with the requirements of BS EN450: 2005 [25] for use with cement, i.e. SiO 2 +A l2O3 +F e2O3 > 70% and SiO2 > 25%. The PFA pozzolanic reactivity was measured in accordance with ASTM C311-87a [26] as explained by Agarwal [27], where the accelerated pozzolanic activity value was found to be 84% (must be greater than 75% [26,27]). ADVA Flow 411 high range water-reducing admixture was used, which is based on a polycar- boxylate polymer next-generation super plasticiser (SP). It has a unit weight of 1.08 kg/l (1080 kg/m 3), a freezing temperature of 0 /C176C, the maximum chloride con- tent is <0.1 wt% and the maximum alkali content is 0.5 wt%. The SCC reference mix was designed by modifying a Normal Vibrated Concrete (NVC) mix design based on the British method that has been explained in Neville [28] and using the mix guidelines given by the European Federation of National Associations Representing producers and applicators of specialist building products for Concrete (EFNARC) [3]. Table 4 shows details of the NVC and reference SCC mix designs. The addition of PFA and SP were used in quantities of 30 wt% and 2.5 wt% of cement, respectively, in order to achieve the 730 mm slump ﬂow value and to meet/ exceed the other SCC requirements (e.g. segregation resistance, V-funnel, etc.), as speciﬁed in EFNARC [3]. The experimental data for fresh SCRC properties, including slump ﬂow, segregation resistance and V-funnel tests, have been published else- where [29]. The rubber aggregate was used to partially substitute Fine Aggregate (FA), Coarse Aggregate (CA), and a 50/50 combination of the two (FCA) in propor- tions of 5, 10, and 15 wt%. In order to produce homogenous mixes, all solid ingre- dients were mixed together in a rotary mixer for three minutes. Afterwards, the mixing water gauged with the full SP quantity was added following the manufac- turer’s recommendations and mixed for a further three minutes. The mixes were al- lowed to rest for two minutes prior to casting, which was done without the aid of vibration or tamping. All specimens for mechanical and dynamic properties were tested at 28-days following water curing at 20 ± 2 /C176C. For compressive and splitting tensile strength, 100 mm cubic moulds were used based on BS 1881-116 [30], and BS 1881:117 [31] respectively and the average of three reading was taken. For ﬂexural and dy- namic properties, a 500 /C2 100 /C2 100 mm prism mould was used and the test for ﬂexural strength, dynamic modulus of elasticity and Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity were carried out in accordance with ASTM C 1018 [32], BS 1881: Part 209 [33] and BS 1881-203 [34] respectively and the average of two reading was recorded. 3. Mechanical properties The mean of three tested values at 28 days was recorded to determine the compressive, splitting tensile and ﬂexural strength, for all mixtures. 3.1. Compressive strength The correlation between unconﬁned compressive strength and both type and amount of rubber aggregate replacement is shown in Fig. 3 . The deteriora

rubber replacement, as shown in Fig. 13 . This is understandable since the addition of crumb rubber causes signiﬁcant air entrain- ment, and the rubber aggregates have a lowE [22,44,45]. The highest reduction in Ed occurred for CR replacement followed by FCR and FR replacement. Fig. 14 shows the relationship between Ed and com- pressive strength for SCRC and NVC, where Ed is signiﬁcantly lower in the rubber-modiﬁed concrete higher showing close agreement with another study using PRC [22]. It is clear that UPV and Ed, as deﬁned by Khaloo et al. [46], de- creases with wt% rubber substitution in Plain Rubberised Concrete (PRC), as highlighted by Najim and Hall [14]. Fig. 15 shows that the UPV reduction in SCRC is highest in CR replacement, followed by FCR and FR. This can be mainly attributed to signiﬁcant absorption of acoustic vibration by the rubber particles compared to mineral aggregates (found to be /C24 21 times lower than hardened cement paste [44]), as well as the increase in bulk porosity as a result of air entrainment. Generally, the UPV was found to reduce in parallel with both compressive strength and Ed, as shown by Fig. 16. Previ- ous studies [47,48] developed generic criteria to assess the quality of rubber-modiﬁed concrete, in relation to the UPV value, against which all of the SCRC mixes tested in this study are classiﬁed as ‘good’ (i.e. UPV > 3.660 km/s). 4.2. Dynamic shear modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) G D and Dynamic Poisson ratio v GD was determined by calculating the ﬂexural fundamental resonant frequency, the value of which is highly dependent upon concrete density and strength as well as aggregate type (see Figs. 17–19 ). GD declines sharply with wt% rubber substitution in a semi-uniform relationship. Experimental data for the dry density values used here have been presented elsewhere [29]. Fig. 20 demonstrates that v is highly dependent upon wt% rub- ber substitution and type (FR, CR, or FCR). As with other dynamic properties, the largest change in v occurred with CR replacement followed by FCR, and with only a minimal change in FR. Clearly coarse aggregate rubber substitution is the most important factor. This correlates with an increase in aggregate speciﬁc surface area which places a requirement on the mix design to have sufﬁcient mortar to encapsulate the remaining coarse particles. 4.3. Vibration damping capacity Damping can be deﬁned as a phenomena resulting in distur- bance and loss of vibration energy due to inelasticity within the transferring body. During acoustic wave propagation through a rigid body, friction is generated at crack interfaces and other imperfections resulting in partial loss of energy. The amount of damping refers to the material quality, where it has been found Fig. 13. The effect of rubber content on Ed. Fig. 14. The relationship between Ed and compressive strength for NVC ⁄ and SCRC ( ⁄ data taken from [52]). Fig. 15. The effect of rubber content on UPV. K.B. Najim, M.R. Hall / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 521–530 527

properties are known to have a dominant inﬂuence on concrete homogeneity [49]. 5. Conclusions Self Compacting Rubberised Concrete (SCRC) has mechanical properties sufﬁcient for structural applications ( fc > 17 MPa; q > 2000 kg/m3), but with superior dynamic properties to plain, NVC or SCC mixes. Crumb rubber aggregate substitution decreased the ﬂexural strength ( Fmax), but resulted in a signiﬁcant improve- ment in strain capacity that led to reduce the Crack Mouth Open Displacement (CMOD), compared to the reference SCC mix, which is important from a serviceability point of view. CMOD ( y) for SCRC can be reliably predicted for a speciﬁed ﬂexural deﬂection ( x), for the mix designs presented here, which could be expanded upon to assist mix design speciﬁcation. The Young’s modulus of elastic- ity ( E) for all SCRC mixes decreased, but resulted in considerable enhancements to ﬂexural toughness, which is important in improving the ductile behaviour and energy absorption. SCRC mixes appear to be highly suitable for specialist applications where dynamic response and vibration damping are a priority. Although Ed decreased with wt% rubber substitution, it was greater than 32 GPa for all replacement types and amounts. UPV measurements can be used as a non-destructive measure to evaluate the quality of SCRC mixes, and a ‘good’ quality rating can be achieved at up to 15 wt% (260 kg/m 3 rubber aggregate) for all replacement types. SCRC mixes exhibit superior vibration damping behaviour, com- pared to SCC and NVC mixes, where both the damping ratio and damping coefﬁcient were enhanced by approximately 230% for SCRC (CR15%) mix. To validate the use of SCRC mix designs in real structural applications, further research is required to determine the durability in terms of freeze–thaw resistance, permeability, and elevated temperature exposure, in addition to large/full scale structural members which must be prepared and tested to simu- late real conditions and assess serviceability. Acknowledgement The authors wish to acknowledge the support of the Iraqi Cul- tural Attaché for the research scholarship enabling this work to be conducted as part of a larger research project. [7] Domone PL. A review of the hardened mechanical properties of self- compacting concrete. Cem Concr Compos 2007;29(1):1–12. [8] Felekoglu B, Türkel S, Baradan B. Experimental investigations on the inﬂuence of paste composition and co

have studied the ﬂexural toughness of rubber-modiﬁed concrete [16,19], and to date none have investigated the behaviour of SCRC. It is deﬁned as the post-crack energy absorption capacity of a material. It represents the calculated area under the ﬂexural load–deﬂection curve up to critical deﬂections, speciﬁed in ASTM C 1018 as d,3 d, 5.5 d, and 10.5 d, where d represents the ﬁrst crack deﬂection or the elastic limit [40]. These values are used to calcu- late the toughness index I5, I10, and I20, as illustrated by Fig. 8 . Ta- ble 5 shows that there is a general tendency for crumb rubber aggregate replacement to signiﬁcantly increase all toughness indi- ces in all cases. The best results for all toughness indices were for 5 wt% FR replacement where the increase was 76% ( I5), 102% ( I10), and 118% ( I20), due to signiﬁcant enhancements in strain capacity and energy absorption. As discussed earlier, this could result from stress relaxation at the rubber–cement interface thus restricting crack mouth opening. From a serviceability point of view, the crack width should not exceed 0.35 mm for interior members, 0.25 mm for exterior mem- ber under normal exposure conditions, and 0.15 mm for external members exposed to aggressive environments in structural con- crete [28]. Therefore, not only is deﬂection against load important, but also the Crack Mouth Opening Displacement (CMOD) needs to be quantiﬁed and understood. For this study, a Digital Image Cor- relation system Q-400 (DIC camera) and ISTRA 4D software were employed during the ﬂexural test to measure the CMOD whilst Table 3 Chemical compositions of PFA. Modulus of Rigidity (i.e. shear modulus), GD, were determined using the resonant frequency tester ERUDIT, based on BS1881-5: 1970 [42], as explained in the ERUDIT technical manual [43].I n addition, the dynamic Poisson ratio, v, was determined by calcula- tion using the term v ¼ Ed 2GD /C0 1 [23]. 4.1. Dynamic modulus of elasticity and Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity (UPV) There is a general but non-precise correlation between modulus of elasticity, E, and compressive strength in concrete [28]. It is also well known that aggregate type and volumetric proportion are highly inﬂuential on strength and E. However, Ed is determined using a non-destructive test with zero applied stress and hence there is neither micro crack formation nor creep during the test. In conse- quence, it appears higher than the static modulus of elasticity (se- cant) and it is almost equal to initial tangent modulus of elasticity, therefore Ed is signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by aggregate type and quan- tity. In this study, Ed was found to decrease systematically with wt% Fig. 10. DIC camera position in four point ﬂexural test. Fig. 11. The relationship between the load and CMOD. Fig. 12. The relationship between the deﬂection and CMOD. 526 K.B. Najim, M.R. Hall / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 521–530

that damping is greater for low strength concrete than high strength such that damping has a positive correlation with ductil- ity rather than brittleness [23,49]. Damping capacity can be evalu- ated by determination of the damping constant, the logarithmic decrement, and the damping ratio. The damping constant (quality factor), Q, is considered to be a more reliable quality indicator of concrete than the dynamic modu- lus due to its sensitivity to any small imperfections, e.g. micro cracks, which causes relatively large changes in Q whilst Ed is only slightly affected [23,43]. Q is calculated from the bandwidth as follows: Q ¼ n0=n1 /C0 n2 ð1Þ where n0 is the resonant frequency (Hz), n1 and n2 are the frequen- cies on each resonant side when the amplitude of vibration is 0.707 times the maximum amplitude. The damping capacity can be as- sessed by the damping coefﬁcient, 1, which is calculated using the following formula [22,50]: 1 ¼ 1 n /C1 ln A0 An ð2Þ where A0 is initial amplitude and An is amplitude after n number of cycles. Logarithmic decrement can also be calculated from the funda- mental resonant frequency and the frequencies on each side, when the vibration amplitude is 0.707 times the maximum amplitude. It is related to Q by the expression 1 = p/Q. The damping ratio f can be calculated using the term f = 1/2p [22,23,49,50]. In general, a low Q-value means higher vibration damping which, in concrete, is mainly affected by the presence of cement paste air voids, particu- larly if water-ﬁlled [51], although in addition the contrast in E for the rubber and cement paste, and the poor ITZ between them, may also play a role. Previous research has also shown that the type of coarse aggregate has greater inﬂuence upon damping than Ed, Fcu, and ﬂexural strength [49]. In this study, the damping constant fell sharply with increased wt% rubber substitution for all replacement types, which is in gen- eral agreement with previous ﬁndings for PRC [22] and thought to be attributable to a weak rubber–cement ITZ and reduced homoge- neity [22]. Fig. 21 shows that FR replacement resulted in the least reduction in damping constant and that CR replacement result in the most, which is supported by the fact that the coarse aggregate Fig. 16. The relationship between UPV and compressive strength for NVC ⁄ and SCRC ( ⁄ data taken from [52]). Fig. 17. The effect of rubber content on GD. Fig. 18. The effect of concrete density on GD. Fig. 19. The effect of compressive strength on GD. Fig. 20. The effect of rubber content on Dynamic Poisson’s ratio. 528 K.B. Najim, M.R. Hall / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 521–530

compressive and splitting tensile strength for both SCC and SCRC. Splitting tensile strength of SCRC can be predicted using the for- mula 0.0535(fcu) + 1886, where Fig. 6 shows the real and predicted values. 3.3. Flexural strength Four point ﬂexural strength testing was conducted to determine the Modulus of Rupture using the equation PL/bd2. From Fig. 4 it can be seen that ﬂexural strength decreases by less than 11% for all types of 5 wt% rubber replacement, but at 15 wt% the reduction was as much as 39% in the case of CR replacement. The addition of rubber decreased the load bearing capacity ( Fmax) due to the positive rela- tion between the strength and ﬁrst peak response, whilst increasing the strain capacity dFmax (deﬂection corresponding to peak load) as shown in Fig. 7 . The maximum deﬂection, for example, increased from 0.039 mm for the SCC reference mix to 0.051 mm for the SCFR10% mix which corresponds to maximum loads of 16.78 and 14.89 MPa, respectively (see Table 5). The reduction in Modulus of Rupture can be explained by the same properties affecting strength, and in addition, crack initiation may take place before the maximum load is applied since micro crack form when the post-peak zone is reached and propagates after exceeding this stage [41]. Therefore, rubber particles may work to delay the formation of micro cracks by stress relaxation. Consequently, crumb rubber modiﬁcation ap- pears to offer measurable improvements to pre-micro crack strain capacity and so would most likely reduce shrinkage cracking in a similarly beneﬁcial manner [16,19]. 3.4. Flexural stiffness The ﬂexural stiffness, K was calculated as the linear part of the load–deﬂection curve between 50% and 10% of the maximum load, deﬁned elsewhere as DF/Dd [19]. It has been determined here, as deﬁned above, and before the ﬁrst crack point initiation which cor- responds to the point at which the load–deﬂection curve becomes non-linear [32], as shown in Fig. 8 . There is a general decline in K with increased rubber content, as shown by Fig. 9 . 3.5. Flexural toughness The ﬂexural toughness indices ( I5, I10, and I20) were determined in accordance with ASTM C 1018 [32] see Fig. 8 . Few researchers Fig. 2.
